docs.text: Babylon CLI A simple CLI for interaction between Cosmotech Products and the cloud

docs.text: A simple CLI for interaction between Cosmotech Products and the cloud
docs.title: Babylon CLI

docs.text: CLI Reference This page provide a reference documentation to our cli babylon CLI used for cloud interactions between CosmoTech and multiple cloud environment Usage: babylon [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]... Options: Name Type Description Default --verbosity , -v text Either CRITICAL, ERROR, WARNING, INFO or DEBUG INFO -e , --environment text N/A . -t , --template text N/A None --help boolean Show this message and exit. False Subcommands api : Group handling communication with the cosmotech API environment : Command group handling local environment information api Group handling communication with the cosmotech API Usage: babylon api [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]... Options: Name Type Description Default --help boolean Show this message and exit. False Subcommands connector : Create a new connector in the API using FILENAME as a template connector Create a new connector in the API using FILENAME as a template Usage: babylon api connector [OPTIONS] FILENAME Options: Name Type Description Default --help boolean Show this message and exit. False environment Command group handling local environment information Usage: babylon environment [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]... Options: Name Type Description Default --help boolean Show this message and exit. False Subcommands complete : Complete the current environment for missing elements display : Display information about the current environment init : Initialize the current environment validate : Validate the current environment complete Complete the current environment for missing elements Usage: babylon environment complete [OPTIONS] Options: Name Type Description Default --help boolean Show this message and exit. False display Display information about the current environment Usage: babylon environment display [OPTIONS] Options: Name Type Description Default --help boolean Show this message and exit. False init Initialize the current environment Usage: babylon environment init [OPTIONS] Options: Name Type Description Default --help boolean Show this message and exit. False validate Validate the current environment Usage: babylon environment validate [OPTIONS] Options: Name Type Description Default --help boolean Show this message and exit. False

docs.text: How-to contribute to Babylon Creating a new group of commands Create a new package inside Babylon.groups This new package comes with a package called commands and you can use the following template for the new_group.__init__.py from click import group from click import pass_context from .commands import list_commands @group() @pass_context def new_group(ctx): """New group of commands""" ctx.obj = dict() # this obj can be passed to the commands of the group by using the click.pass_obj decorator for _command in list_commands: new_group.add_command(_command) Initialize the commands.__init__.py You can use the following template to initialize the new_group.commands.__init__.py list_commands = [] Add your group to the groups callable by the cli You have to add an import for your group in the Babylon.groups.__init__.py from .api import api from .new_group import new_group # Add your import here command_groups = [ api, new_group, # Add your group command to the list of existing commands ] And your new group is then ready to be called babylon new-group #Usage: babylon new-group [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]... # # New group of commands # #Options: # --help Show this message and exit. # #Commands: # my_command Doc-string for my new command Adding a new command to an existing group Template This template can be copied in the commands package for the group we want to add the command to. from click import command from click import pass_obj from Babylon.utils.decorators import timing_decorator import logging logger = logging.getLogger("Babylon") @command() @pass_obj @timing_decorator def my_command(ctx): """Doc-string for my new command""" pass Add to commands.__init__.py Once the command is created you can make a link to it in the commands.__init__.py file from .older_command import older_command from .my_command import my_command # You import your new command here list_commands = [ older_command, my_command, # And you add it to the list of existing commands here ] And that's all folks, you added your command to an existing group of commands
docs.title: How-to contribute to Babylon
Creating a new group of commands

docs.text: This new package comes with a package called commands and you can use the following template for the new_group.__init__.py from click import group from click import pass_context from .commands import list_commands @group() @pass_context def new_group(ctx): """New group of commands""" ctx.obj = dict() # this obj can be passed to the commands of the group by using the click.pass_obj decorator for _command in list_commands: new_group.add_command(_command)
docs.title: Create a new package inside Babylon.groups

docs.text: You can use the following template to initialize the new_group.commands.__init__.py list_commands = []
docs.title: Initialize the commands.__init__.py

docs.text: You have to add an import for your group in the Babylon.groups.__init__.py from .api import api from .new_group import new_group # Add your import here command_groups = [ api, new_group, # Add your group command to the list of existing commands ] And your new group is then ready to be called babylon new-group #Usage: babylon new-group [OPTIONS] COMMAND [ARGS]... # # New group of commands # #Options: # --help Show this message and exit. # #Commands: # my_command Doc-string for my new command
docs.title: Add your group to the groups callable by the cli
Adding a new command to an existing group

docs.text: This template can be copied in the commands package for the group we want to add the command to. from click import command from click import pass_obj from Babylon.utils.decorators import timing_decorator import logging logger = logging.getLogger("Babylon") @command() @pass_obj @timing_decorator def my_command(ctx): """Doc-string for my new command""" pass
docs.title: Template

docs.text: Once the command is created you can make a link to it in the commands.__init__.py file from .older_command import older_command from .my_command import my_command # You import your new command here list_commands = [ older_command, my_command, # And you add it to the list of existing commands here ] And that's all folks, you added your command to an existing group of commands
docs.title: Add to commands.__init__.py

docs.text: About the environment Minimal environment Environment ├── deploy.yaml └── API └── API_FILES_HERE API This is a folder containing the different yaml files you will want to send to the api For example: - Solution.yaml - Workspace.yaml Deploy.yaml This is a yaml file containing a list of key-values that can be used around every commands
docs.title: About the environment

docs.text: Environment ├── deploy.yaml └── API └── API_FILES_HERE
docs.title: Minimal environment

docs.text: This is a folder containing the different yaml files you will want to send to the api For example: - Solution.yaml - Workspace.yaml
docs.title: API

docs.text: This is a yaml file containing a list of key-values that can be used around every commands
docs.title: Deploy.yaml

docs.text: Organization of the repository Babylon : Core package ├── __init__.py : init file for the core package ├── main.py : Contains main function for the cli ├── version.py : Contains version number for the cli ├── groups : Groups package │ ├── __init__.py : init file for the groups, contains list of all existing groups │ └── <GROUP_NAME> : Package for a given command group │ ├── __init__.py : init file for given command group, contains declaration of the click.group and add commands to it │ └── commands : Package for commands of a group │ ├── __init__.py : init file for the package, contains list of all commands in the package │ └── <COMMAND_NAME>.py : python file containing the code for the actual command └── utils : Utils package ├── <UTIL_NAME>.py : Util python file └── __init__.py : init file for the utils package
docs.title: Organization of the repository
